overall_summary: American Terawatt argues that the main barrier to powering AI data centers is misaligned utility incentives and slow interconnection, not a lack of capital. It proposes that grid-responsive AI data centers throttle GPU power during grid peaks to act as flexible loads, and it asks FERC to direct ISOs and RTOs to create expedited, nondiscriminatory interconnection processes and tariffs for defined large flexible loads, funded by up-front premiums so existing ratepayers are not burdened.

quotes.text: American Terawatt urges the Commission to focus swiftly and pragmatically on reforms that can be adopted without delay and that will encourage the timely expansion of digital and energy infrastructure.
quotes.concern: Urges fast, pragmatic interconnection reforms to expand digital and energy infrastructure

quotes.text: Traditional interconnection of large loads averages five years, and often up to eight years throughout the country. Our ability to bring compute for AI online is severely constrained by the availability of power.
quotes.concern: Says large-load interconnection takes five to eight years and constrains AI buildout

quotes.text: To secure power more quickly, data-center developers are increasingly resorting to expensive, risky solutions such as building and operating their own behind-the-meter natural gas power plants colocated with large datacenter campuses.
quotes.concern: Notes developers turn to risky behind-the-meter colocated gas plants to get power faster

quotes.text: The core issue is not capital. The real constraint is incentives. Utilities operate under cost-of-service regulation, where capital expenditures must be approved by regulators and then added to “rate base” to earn a regulated return.
quotes.concern: Argues cost-of-service rate-base incentives, not capital, are the core constraint

quotes.text: This structure rewards utilities for undertaking larger, slower, and more capital-intensive projects, because those projects expand rate base, even when smaller or faster solutions would deliver usable grid capacity sooner.
quotes.concern: Says rate-base structure rewards slower, larger projects over faster cheaper solutions

quotes.text: Existing off-peak grid capacity could accommodate an additional 100 GW of flexible loads, more than enough to cover data-center growth and other demand through 2028 and beyond, without needing new power plants.
quotes.concern: Claims off-peak capacity could host 100 GW of flexible loads without new generation

quotes.text: by throttling GPU power and slowing computations rather than switching devices off entirely, grid-responsive AI data centers (GRAIDs) can maintain uptime SLAs without ever stopping workloads.
quotes.concern: Proposes GPU power throttling so flexible data centers keep uptime SLAs during curtailment

quotes.text: Including facility overhead, a 100-megawatt (MW) GRAIDs facility could curtail approximately 50 MW on demand, all while keeping workloads continuously running.
quotes.concern: Estimates a 100 MW grid-responsive facility can curtail about 50 MW on demand

quotes.text: Data-center developers willing to build flexible AI factories or retrofit existing sites with flexible load capabilities should have access to a significantly accelerated grid interconnection process.
quotes.concern: Asks that flexible data centers get a significantly accelerated interconnection process

quotes.text: By charging a predetermined up-front premium for this form of accelerated interconnection, utilities need not incur increased rates for consumers in the short term.
quotes.concern: Proposes an up-front premium for expedited interconnection so existing ratepayers are not charged

quotes.text: The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) has so far explicitly chosen not to regulate flexible loads, leaving them to individual grid operators. It’s time for that to change.
quotes.concern: Argues FERC should begin regulating flexible loads rather than leaving them to grid operators

quotes.text: FERC should direct independent system operators (ISOs) to develop expedited interconnection processes for defined large flexible loads, with transparent criteria.
quotes.concern: Recommends FERC direct ISOs to build expedited interconnection processes for flexible loads

quotes.text: FERC should direct regional transmission organizations and ISOs to propose enabling, nondiscriminatory tariffs for participation in expedited interconnection processes for defined large flexible loads.
quotes.concern: Recommends FERC direct RTOs and ISOs to propose nondiscriminatory expedited-interconnection tariffs

quotes.text: To ensure clarity for both developers and grid operators and prevent a national patchwork of regulations, FERC should define qualifying flexible loads for the purposes of transmission-level interconnection and wholesale market participation.
quotes.concern: Asks FERC to define qualifying flexible loads to prevent a state-by-state regulatory patchwork

bins.name: Expedited interconnection for flexible loads
bins.description: American Terawatt says traditional large-load interconnection takes five to eight years and asks FERC to direct ISOs and RTOs to create expedited interconnection processes and enabling tariffs for defined large flexible loads.

bins.name: Flexible loads as the core reform
bins.description: The filer centers its proposal on flexible loads, arguing off-peak capacity could host 100 GW and that grid-responsive data centers can throttle GPU power to curtail roughly half their draw without stopping workloads. It urges FERC to define qualifying flexible loads and regulate them rather than leaving the matter to individual grid operators.

bins.name: Rate-base incentives and up-front premium
bins.description: American Terawatt argues cost-of-service rate-base regulation, not capital, drives slow and oversized projects. It proposes a predetermined up-front premium for accelerated interconnection so existing ratepayers face no short-term rate increase.

bins.name: Behind-the-meter colocation as a stopgap
bins.description: The filer characterizes developer-owned behind-the-meter gas plants colocated with data centers as expensive and risky, framing them as an untenable workaround that grid-based flexible interconnection should replace.

bins.name: FERC should regulate flexible loads
bins.description: The filer says FERC has so far declined to regulate flexible loads and should now define qualifying flexible loads for transmission-level interconnection and wholesale market participation to avoid a national patchwork.

bins.name: Premium funds upgrades, not ratepayers
bins.description: American Terawatt proposes that an up-front premium paid by developers for accelerated interconnection cover the associated costs, so existing consumers do not bear short-term rate increases.

bins.name: GPU power throttling for curtailment
bins.description: The filer proposes grid-responsive AI data centers that throttle GPU power on demand-response signals instead of shutting devices off, so a 100 MW facility could curtail about 50 MW while keeping workloads running and uptime SLAs intact.
